metadata.description: Error classification, dependency tracing, and fix ordering for Java 17+ / Kotlin 1.9+ projects with Gradle and Maven support

categories.name: Symbol Resolution Errors
categories.description: Errors about finding classes, methods, variables, and packages

errors.rootCause: The compiler cannot find {symbolKind} '{symbolName}'. This means the symbol is not imported, not declared in the current scope, or does not exist in the referenced type.
errors.prescription:
1. Check if '{symbolName}' needs an import statement — add 'import package.{symbolName};' at the top of the file.
2. If it is a local variable or method, check for typos in the name.
3. If it is from a dependency, ensure the dependency is declared in build.gradle or pom.xml.
4. If it was recently renamed or moved, update the reference.

conditions.rootCause: Class '{symbolName}' is not imported or does not exist on the classpath. This usually means a missing import statement or a missing dependency.
conditions.prescription: Add 'import <package>.{symbolName};' to the file. If the class is from an external library, add the library to your build.gradle or pom.xml dependencies.

conditions.rootCause: Method '{symbolName}' does not exist on the target type or is not accessible from the current context.
conditions.prescription:
1. Check the method name for typos.
2. Verify the method exists in the class you are calling it on.
3. If it is a static method, ensure you have a static import or use ClassName.{symbolName}().
4. Check if the method was removed or renamed in a newer version of the library.

conditions.rootCause: Variable '{symbolName}' is not declared in the current scope. It may be misspelled or not yet initialized.
conditions.prescription:
1. Check for typos in the variable name.
2. Ensure the variable is declared before it is used.
3. Check variable scope — it may be declared inside a block (if/for/try) and not accessible outside.

errors.rootCause: Package '{packageName}' does not exist on the classpath. The dependency providing this package is either missing from build configuration or the package name is incorrect.
errors.prescription:
1. If '{packageName}' is from an external library, add the dependency to build.gradle or pom.xml.
2. If it is a project module, ensure the module is included in settings.gradle and declared as a dependency.
3. Check the package name for typos.
4. Run 'gradle dependencies' or 'mvn dependency:tree' to verify the dependency is resolved.

conditions.rootCause: Package '{packageName}' was part of Java EE which was removed from the JDK in Java 11+. It must now be added as an explicit dependency.
conditions.prescription: Add the Jakarta EE or javax dependency explicitly. For example, for javax.annotation, add 'implementation "jakarta.annotation:jakarta.annotation-api:2.1.1"' to build.gradle.

conditions.rootCause: Package '{packageName}' is part of Jakarta EE. The Jakarta dependency is not on the classpath.
conditions.prescription: Add the appropriate Jakarta EE dependency to your build configuration. Jakarta packages replaced javax packages starting with Jakarta EE 9.

errors.rootCause: Method '{methodName}({paramTypes})' does not exist on the target type. The method name may be misspelled, the parameter types may be wrong, or the method may not be defined.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the method name '{methodName}' for typos.
2. Verify the parameter types match — you may need to cast arguments or use a different overload.
3. If the method should exist, add it to the target class.
4. Check if the method was renamed or removed in a library update.

errors.messagePattern: constructor\s+(?<className>\w+)\s+in class\s+(?<fullClass>[\w.]+)\s+cannot be applied to given types
errors.rootCause: No constructor in '{fullClass}' matches the given arguments. The class may not have a matching constructor, or the argument types are incorrect.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the constructor parameters of '{className}' and ensure the argument types match.
2. If no matching constructor exists, add one to the class or use a factory method.
3. Check if a no-arg constructor is needed — if any constructor is defined, the default no-arg constructor is not auto-generated.

errors.messagePattern: method\s+(?<methodName>\w+)\s+in class\s+(?<className>[\w.]+)\s+cannot be applied to given types
errors.rootCause: Method '{methodName}' in class '{className}' cannot be applied to the provided argument types. The method signature does not match the arguments passed.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the expected parameter types for '{methodName}' in '{className}'.
2. Cast or convert arguments to match the expected types.
3. If using generics, ensure the type parameters are compatible.
4. Check for a different overload that matches your arguments.

errors.rootCause: No overload of '{methodName}' accepts the argument types ({argTypes}). None of the available overloads match the provided arguments.
errors.prescription:
1. Review all overloads of '{methodName}' and determine which one you intend to call.
2. Cast arguments to the expected types.
3. If no suitable overload exists, you may need to add one.

errors.rootCause: Field '{memberName}' does not exist on type '{typeName}'. The field may be misspelled, private, or not yet declared.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the spelling of '{memberName}'.
2. If the field is private, use a getter method instead.
3. Add the field to '{typeName}' if it should exist.
4. If accessing through inheritance, ensure the field is protected or public in the parent class.

categories.name: Type Compatibility Errors
categories.description: Errors about type mismatches, incompatible assignments, and type conversion failures

errors.rootCause: Type '{actualType}' cannot be converted to '{expectedType}'. These types are not assignment-compatible in Java's type system.
errors.prescription:
1. If the conversion is valid at runtime, use an explicit cast: ({expectedType}) value.
2. If the types are related by inheritance, check if you need to change the variable type or add an interface.
3. If using generics, check that type parameters are compatible.
4. Consider using a conversion method (e.g., toString(), intValue()).

conditions.rootCause: Optional<{expectedType}> cannot be directly assigned to {expectedType}. You must unwrap the Optional first.
conditions.prescription: Use .get(), .orElse(default), .orElseThrow(), or .orElseGet(() -> default) to unwrap the Optional value.

conditions.rootCause: The collection type does not match. Java generics are invariant: List<Child> is not assignable to List<Parent>.
conditions.prescription: Use wildcards: change the expected type to List<? extends {actualType}> if you only read, or List<? super {actualType}> if you only write.

errors.rootCause: Converting from '{fromType}' to '{toType}' may lose information (e.g., double to int loses decimal precision, long to int may overflow).
errors.prescription:
1. Add an explicit cast: ({toType}) value — this acknowledges the potential data loss.
2. Use Math.toIntExact() for long-to-int if you want overflow protection.
3. Consider if you should keep the wider type instead.

errors.rootCause: Expected type '{expectedType}' but found '{actualType}'. The expression produces a value of the wrong type.
errors.prescription:
1. Cast the value to '{expectedType}' if appropriate.
2. Change the return type or variable type.
3. Use a conversion method.

errors.rootCause: Casting to a generic type cannot be verified at runtime due to type erasure. The cast from '{fromType}' to '{toType}' may fail at runtime with a ClassCastException.
errors.prescription:
1. Add @SuppressWarnings("unchecked") if you are certain the cast is safe.
2. Restructure to avoid raw types — use properly parameterized generics.
3. Use instanceof checks before casting where possible.

errors.rootCause: A raw type is being used where a parameterized type is expected. This is unsafe because it bypasses generic type checking.
errors.prescription:
1. Add the missing type parameter: use List<String> instead of raw List.
2. If interacting with legacy code, add @SuppressWarnings("unchecked").

errors.messagePattern: type argument\s+(?<typeArg>[\w.<>]+)\s+is not within bounds of type-variable\s+(?<typeVar>\w+)
errors.rootCause: Type argument '{typeArg}' does not satisfy the bound declared on type variable '{typeVar}'. The type must extend or implement the required bound.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the generic declaration to see the bound on '{typeVar}'.
2. Make '{typeArg}' implement/extend the required interface or class.
3. Use a different type argument that satisfies the bound.

errors.messagePattern: (?:cannot infer type arguments for|incompatible types: inference variable)\s+(?<typeName>[\w.<>]+)
errors.rootCause: The compiler cannot infer the type arguments for '{typeName}' from the context. The types involved are ambiguous or conflicting.
errors.prescription:
1. Provide explicit type arguments: new ClassName<Type1, Type2>().
2. Add a type annotation to the variable receiving the result.
3. Check that the arguments passed resolve to compatible types.

categories.name: Access Control Errors
categories.description: Errors about private, protected, and package-private access violations

errors.rootCause: '{symbolName}' has {accessLevel} access in '{typeName}' and cannot be accessed from the current context.
errors.prescription:
1. If you own '{typeName}', change the access modifier of '{symbolName}' to public (or protected if accessing from a subclass).
2. Use a public getter/setter method instead of accessing the field directly.
3. If in the same package, package-private access should work — check your package declarations.

conditions.rootCause: '{symbolName}' is private in '{typeName}' and can only be accessed within '{typeName}' itself.
conditions.prescription: Use a public getter/setter method, or change the access modifier to protected/public. If this is a design issue, consider the encapsulation implications.

conditions.rootCause: '{symbolName}' is protected in '{typeName}' and can only be accessed from subclasses or within the same package.
conditions.prescription: Extend '{typeName}' to access the protected member, move your code to the same package, or change the modifier to public.

errors.rootCause: '{symbolName}' is not visible from the current compilation unit. It has restricted access (private, package-private, or protected).
errors.prescription:
1. Check the access modifier of '{symbolName}' and widen it if appropriate.
2. Move your code to the same package if package-private access is intended.
3. Use a public API method instead of accessing the symbol directly.

categories.name: Class Structure Errors
categories.description: Errors about class hierarchy, abstract methods, overriding, and duplicate definitions

errors.messagePattern: (?<className>\w+)\s+is not abstract and does not override abstract method\s+(?<methodName>\w+)\((?<paramTypes>[^)]*)\)\s+in\s+(?<parentClass>[\w.]+)
errors.match: error:.*is not abstract and does not override abstract method
errors.rootCause: Class '{className}' extends/implements '{parentClass}' but does not implement the abstract method '{methodName}({paramTypes})'.
errors.prescription:
1. Implement the method '{methodName}({paramTypes})' in '{className}' with an @Override annotation.
2. Alternatively, declare '{className}' as abstract if it should not provide an implementation.
3. Use your IDE's 'Implement methods' feature to auto-generate stubs.

errors.rootCause: Class '{className}' is defined in multiple files. Java requires each public class to be in exactly one file matching its name.
errors.prescription:
1. Remove the duplicate class definition from one of the files.
2. If both definitions are intentional, rename one or move them to different packages.
3. Check that you don't have the same file in multiple source sets (main + test).

errors.rootCause: A non-void method does not return a value on all code paths. Every branch (if/else, switch, try/catch) must end with a return statement.
errors.prescription:
1. Add a return statement at the end of the method.
2. Ensure all branches (if/else, switch cases, catch blocks) return a value.
3. If the method should not return a value, change the return type to void.

errors.rootCause: Code appears after a return, throw, break, or continue statement and will never be executed.
errors.prescription:
1. Remove the unreachable code.
2. If the code should execute, move it before the return/throw/break/continue statement.
3. Check if the preceding return or throw is in the wrong place.

errors.messagePattern: method does not override or implement a method from a supertype
errors.match: error:.*method does not override or implement a method from a supertype
errors.rootCause: A method annotated with @Override does not actually override any method in a superclass or interface. The method signature may be wrong, or the parent class may have changed.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the method signature matches the parent exactly — name, parameters, and return type.
2. If the parent method was renamed or removed, update this method accordingly.
3. Remove the @Override annotation if this method is not intended to override anything.

errors.rootCause: Method '{methodName}' in '{parentClass}' is declared final and cannot be overridden in '{childClass}'.
errors.prescription:
1. Do not override the final method — use composition instead of inheritance.
2. If you control the parent class, remove the 'final' modifier.
3. Use a different method name and call the parent method from within it.

errors.rootCause: Class '{className}' is declared final and cannot be extended. Final classes are sealed against inheritance.
errors.prescription:
1. Use composition instead of inheritance — hold an instance of '{className}' as a field.
2. If you control '{className}', remove the 'final' modifier.
3. If '{className}' implements an interface, program against the interface instead.

errors.rootCause: '{className}' is an abstract class and cannot be instantiated directly with 'new'.
errors.prescription:
1. Instantiate a concrete subclass of '{className}' instead.
2. Use an anonymous class: new {className}() { /* implement abstract methods */ }.
3. Use a factory method if one is provided.

errors.rootCause: '{interfaceName}' is an interface and cannot be instantiated directly.
errors.prescription:
1. Use a concrete class that implements '{interfaceName}'.
2. For functional interfaces, use a lambda: () -> { /* impl */ }.
3. Use an anonymous class implementation.

errors.messagePattern: class is not allowed to extend sealed class:\s*(?<sealedClass>[\w.]+)
errors.match: error:.*not allowed to extend sealed class
errors.rootCause: The sealed class '{sealedClass}' does not permit this class in its 'permits' clause. Only explicitly listed classes can extend a sealed class.
errors.prescription:
1. Add your class to the 'permits' clause of '{sealedClass}'.
2. If you do not control '{sealedClass}', use composition instead.
3. Ensure your subclass is in the same package or module as the sealed class.
errors.codeBlock:
// In the sealed class:
public sealed class Shape permits Circle, Rectangle { }

categories.name: Syntax and Parse Errors
categories.description: Errors in code structure, missing tokens, unexpected tokens

errors.rootCause: A semicolon is missing at the end of a statement.
errors.prescription: Add a semicolon at the indicated position. Check for multi-line statements that may need a semicolon on the previous line.

errors.rootCause: A '{bracket}' bracket is expected but missing. The code has an unmatched opening or closing bracket.
errors.prescription:
1. Check for unmatched brackets in the surrounding code.
2. Use your editor's bracket matching feature to find the mismatch.
3. The error may be caused by a missing bracket several lines above.

errors.rootCause: The compiler encountered an unexpected token where an expression was expected. Common causes: access modifier in wrong place, missing operator, or stray characters.
errors.prescription:
1. Check for access modifiers (public/private) inside a method — they are only valid at class level.
2. Look for missing operators or stray characters.
3. Ensure the previous line is complete (no missing semicolons).

errors.rootCause: The compiler expected a type declaration but found something unexpected. This often happens when code appears outside a class body.
errors.prescription:
1. Ensure all code is inside a class, interface, or enum.
2. Check for extra closing braces that end the class prematurely.
3. Verify the class/method structure is well-formed.

errors.messagePattern: class, interface, enum, or record expected
errors.match: error:.*class, interface, enum, or record expected
errors.rootCause: Code appears at the top level outside any class, interface, enum, or record. In Java, all code must be inside a type declaration (except package and import statements).
errors.prescription:
1. Ensure the file has a proper class/interface declaration wrapping the code.
2. Check for an extra closing brace '}' that prematurely closes the class.
3. Count braces to find the mismatch.

errors.rootCause: A name (identifier) is expected at this position but something else was found. Common cause: using a Java keyword as a variable/method name.
errors.prescription:
1. Check if you used a reserved keyword (class, int, return, etc.) as a variable name.
2. Add the missing identifier.
3. Check for syntax errors on the preceding line.

errors.rootCause: A string literal was opened with a double quote but not closed on the same line.
errors.prescription:
1. Add the closing double quote.
2. For multi-line strings in Java 17+, use text blocks: """multi-line text""".
3. Escape any internal double quotes with backslash.

errors.rootCause: An 'else' clause appears without a preceding 'if'. This usually happens when a semicolon is placed after the if-block or braces are mismatched.
errors.prescription:
1. Remove any semicolon after the if statement's closing parenthesis.
2. Ensure the if-block's closing brace immediately precedes the 'else'.
3. Check brace matching.

errors.rootCause: Variable '{varName}' is declared twice in method '{methodName}'. Java does not allow duplicate variable names in the same scope.
errors.prescription:
1. Rename one of the declarations.
2. If you want to reassign, remove the type declaration and just assign: {varName} = newValue.
3. Check if the variable should be in a different scope (inside a nested block).

categories.name: Null Safety and Resource Errors
categories.description: Errors about null pointers, resource leaks, and static analysis warnings

errors.rootCause: A value that may be null is being used without a null check. This can cause a NullPointerException at runtime.
errors.prescription:
1. Add a null check before accessing the value: if (value != null) { ... }
2. Use Optional<T> to make nullability explicit.
3. Use Objects.requireNonNull() at method entry for fail-fast behavior.
4. Consider using @Nullable and @NonNull annotations.

errors.messagePattern: (?<resourceType>[\w.]+)\s+(?:is never closed|should be managed by try-with-resources)
errors.rootCause: A resource of type '{resourceType}' that implements AutoCloseable is opened but never properly closed. This can leak file handles, connections, or memory.
'{symbolName}' in '{className}' has been deprecated and may be removed in a future version.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the @Deprecated annotation's javadoc for a recommended replacement.
2. Look for @deprecated tag in the javadoc explaining what to use instead.
3. If no replacement exists, add @SuppressWarnings("deprecation") to suppress the warning.

errors.rootCause: Variable '{varName}' may be used before it is assigned a value. Not all code paths guarantee initialization.

errors.messagePattern: exception\s+(?<exceptionType>[\w.]+)\s+is never thrown in body of corresponding try statement
errors.rootCause: The catch clause for '{exceptionType}' is unreachable because no code in the try block throws this checked exception.
errors.prescription:
1. Remove the unnecessary catch clause for '{exceptionType}'.
2. If the code was recently changed, the exception may no longer be thrown.
3. If you need the catch for future use, catch a broader exception type.

errors.messagePattern: unreported exception\s+(?<exceptionType>[\w.]+);\s+must be caught or declared to be thrown
errors.rootCause: The checked exception '{exceptionType}' is thrown but not handled. Java requires checked exceptions to be either caught or declared in the method signature.
errors.prescription:
1. Wrap the throwing code in a try-catch block.
2. Add 'throws {exceptionType}' to the method signature.
3. Convert to an unchecked exception: throw new RuntimeException(e).

categories.name: Kotlin-Specific Errors
categories.description: Errors specific to the Kotlin compiler

errors.rootCause: Symbol '{symbolName}' cannot be resolved. It is not imported, not declared in the current scope, or does not exist.
errors.prescription:
1. Add an import for '{symbolName}': import package.{symbolName}
2. Check the symbol name for typos.
3. If it is from a library, ensure the dependency is in build.gradle.kts.
4. For Java interop, ensure the Java class is public and on the classpath.

conditions.rootCause: Android resource class 'R' is unresolved. This usually means the project has not been built, or there are errors in XML resource files.
conditions.prescription:
1. Build the project to regenerate the R class.
2. Check for errors in XML layout, values, or manifest files.
3. Ensure the correct package is imported for R.

errors.rootCause: The inferred type is '{actualType}' but '{expectedType}' was expected. The types are not compatible in Kotlin's type system.
errors.prescription:
1. Check if the value needs a type conversion (e.g., .toString(), .toInt()).
2. If the types are related by inheritance, use an explicit cast: value as {expectedType}.
3. For nullable vs non-nullable, use !! (assert non-null) or safe call (?.).
4. Check generic type parameters for variance issues.

conditions.rootCause: A nullable type '{actualType}' is being used where a non-nullable '{expectedType}' is required.
conditions.prescription:
1. Use the safe call operator: value?.method()
2. Use the Elvis operator: value ?: defaultValue
3. Use the non-null assertion: value!! (only if you are certain it is not null)
4. Add a null check: if (value != null) { ... }

errors.messagePattern: [Oo]nly safe \(\.\?\) or non-null asserted \(!!\) calls are allowed on a nullable receiver of type\s+(?<nullableType>[\w.<>?]+)
errors.match: e:.*[Oo]nly safe.*or non-null asserted.*calls are allowed
errors.rootCause: A method or property is being called on a nullable receiver of type '{nullableType}'. Kotlin's null safety system requires explicit handling of nullable values.

errors.messagePattern: [Ss]mart cast to '(?<targetType>[\w.<>]+)' is impossible,? because '(?<varName>[\w.]+)' is a (?<reason>mutable property|var|property that has open or custom getter)
errors.rootCause: Smart cast to '{targetType}' is impossible because '{varName}' is a {reason}. The value could change between the null check and the usage, so the compiler cannot guarantee safety.
errors.prescription:
1. Capture the value in a local val: val local = {varName}; then check and use local.
2. Use an explicit cast: ({varName} as {targetType}).method()
3. Use safe cast: ({varName} as? {targetType})?.method()
4. Use let: {varName}?.let { it.method() }

errors.rootCause: Function '{methodName}' is marked with 'override' but does not override any function in a superclass or interface. The signature may not match, or the parent method may have been removed.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the method name and parameter types match the parent exactly.
2. If the parent method was removed or renamed, update this override accordingly.
3. Remove the 'override' keyword if this is a new method.
4. Ensure the superclass is correctly specified in the class declaration.

errors.rootCause: A 'val' (read-only) property or variable is being reassigned. In Kotlin, 'val' is immutable after initialization.
errors.prescription:
1. Change 'val' to 'var' if the variable needs to be mutable.
2. If immutability is desired, restructure the code to not reassign.
3. Use a new variable for the new value.

errors.messagePattern: Class '(?<className>\w+)' is not abstract and does not implement abstract member\s+(?<memberDesc>.+)
errors.match: e:.*is not abstract and does not implement abstract member
errors.rootCause: Class '{className}' must implement all abstract members from its superclass/interface, but '{memberDesc}' is missing.
errors.prescription:
1. Implement the missing member in '{className}'.
2. Or mark '{className}' as abstract: abstract class {className}.
3. Use your IDE's 'Implement members' feature to auto-generate stubs.

errors.rootCause: Required parameter '{paramName}' was not provided when calling a function or constructor.
errors.prescription:
1. Pass a value for '{paramName}' in the call.
2. If the parameter should be optional, add a default value in the function definition: paramName: Type = defaultValue.
3. Use named arguments for clarity: functionCall(paramName = value).

errors.messagePattern: [Rr]eturn type is '(?<actualType>[\w.<>?]+)', which is not a subtype of overridden.*'(?<expectedType>[\w.<>?]+)'
errors.match: e:.*[Rr]eturn type is.*not a subtype of overridden
errors.rootCause: The return type '{actualType}' of the overriding function is not compatible with the expected return type '{expectedType}' declared in the parent.
errors.prescription:
1. Change the return type to match the parent: '{expectedType}'.
2. If you need a more specific return type, ensure it is a subtype of '{expectedType}'.
3. Check if the parent method's return type was changed recently.

errors.rootCause: The overriding member '{memberName}' has a more restrictive visibility than the parent's '{parentVisibility}'. Kotlin does not allow narrowing visibility on overrides.
errors.prescription:
1. Change the override's visibility to match or widen the parent's '{parentVisibility}'.
2. If the parent is 'public', the override must also be 'public'.

errors.messagePattern: [Dd]ata class must have at least one primary constructor parameter
errors.match: e:.*[Dd]ata class must have at least one primary constructor parameter
errors.rootCause: A data class must have at least one property in its primary constructor. Empty data classes are not allowed in Kotlin.
errors.prescription:
1. Add at least one 'val' or 'var' parameter to the primary constructor.
2. If the class truly has no properties, use a regular class instead of a data class.

errors.rootCause: A sealed class or sealed interface cannot be directly instantiated. Only its subclasses can be instantiated.
errors.prescription:
1. Instantiate one of the sealed class's subclasses instead.
2. If no subclass exists yet, create one.
3. Sealed classes are used for restricted class hierarchies — each variant is a subclass.

errors.messagePattern: Extension is shadowed by a member
errors.match: w:.*[Ee]xtension is shadowed by a member
errors.rootCause: An extension function has the same name and signature as a member function. The member function always takes priority over the extension.
errors.prescription:
1. Rename the extension function to avoid the conflict.
2. Remove the extension if the member function provides the same behavior.
3. Be aware that the member function will always be called — the extension is effectively dead code.

errors.rootCause: A Java type is being used as a platform type (T!). Kotlin cannot determine nullability for Java types without @Nullable/@NonNull annotations.
errors.prescription:
1. Add explicit type annotations to clarify nullability: val x: String? = javaMethod().
2. Add @Nullable or @NonNull annotations to the Java code.
3. Use safe calls when accessing platform types: javaObj?.method().

errors.messagePattern: [Ss]uspend function '(?<funcName>\w+)' should be called only from a coroutine or another suspend function
errors.match: e:.*[Ss]uspend function.*should be called only from a coroutine
errors.rootCause: Suspend function '{funcName}' is being called from a non-suspend context. Suspend functions can only be called from coroutines or other suspend functions.
errors.prescription:
1. Mark the calling function as 'suspend'.
2. Launch a coroutine: launch { {funcName}() } or runBlocking { {funcName}() }.
3. Use a coroutine scope: viewModelScope.launch { {funcName}() }.

errors.rootCause: Attempting to access a companion object member incorrectly. In Kotlin, companion object members are accessed directly through the class name, not through '.Companion'.
errors.prescription:
1. Access the member directly: ClassName.memberName (not ClassName.Companion.memberName).
2. From Java code, use ClassName.Companion.getMember() or add @JvmStatic to the Kotlin companion member.

categories.name: Build Tool Errors (Gradle / Maven)
categories.description: Errors from Gradle and Maven build systems

errors.rootCause: Gradle cannot resolve dependency '{dependency}'. The artifact may not exist in the configured repositories, or the version may be wrong.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the group:artifact:version string for typos in build.gradle.
2. Ensure the repository containing '{dependency}' is declared (mavenCentral(), google(), etc.).
3. Check if the version exists on Maven Central or the appropriate repository.
4. Run 'gradle --refresh-dependencies' to clear the cache.

conditions.rootCause: SNAPSHOT dependency '{dependency}' cannot be resolved. SNAPSHOT artifacts require a repository that hosts snapshots.

errors.messagePattern: Could not determine the dependencies of task '(?<taskName>[^']+)'
errors.match: Could not determine the dependencies of task
errors.rootCause: Gradle configuration failed for task '{taskName}'. This usually means a dependency could not be resolved during the configuration phase.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the full error output for the underlying cause (usually a missing dependency).
2. Run 'gradle dependencies' to identify resolution failures.
3. Check build.gradle for syntax errors or invalid dependency declarations.

errors.rootCause: The Gradle build script has a syntax error at line {line}. The Groovy/Kotlin DSL cannot be compiled.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the build.gradle(.kts) file at line {line} for syntax errors.
2. For Kotlin DSL, ensure proper Kotlin syntax (parentheses, string templates).
3. For Groovy DSL, check for missing quotes or incorrect closures.

errors.messagePattern: (?:Cannot access class|does not have access to).*from module.*dependency
errors.match: (?:Cannot access class|does not have access to).*module
errors.rootCause: A class from a transitive dependency is not accessible because it was declared with 'implementation' scope in a dependency module. 'implementation' dependencies are not exposed to consumers.
errors.prescription:
1. In the module that declares the dependency, change 'implementation' to 'api' to expose it to consumers.
2. Or add the dependency directly to the consuming module's build.gradle.
3. Use 'api' only when the dependency is part of your module's public API.

errors.rootCause: Maven cannot find artifact {groupId}:{artifactId}:{version} in any configured repository.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the groupId, artifactId, and version for typos in pom.xml.
2. Search for the correct coordinates on https://search.maven.org.
3. Add the repository that hosts the artifact if it is not on Maven Central.
4. Run 'mvn dependency:resolve' to diagnose.
errors.codeBlock:
<!-- pom.xml -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>{groupId}</groupId>
    <artifactId>{artifactId}</artifactId>
    <version>{version}</version>
</dependency>

errors.rootCause: Maven plugin '{pluginId}' cannot be found in the configured repositories.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the plugin groupId, artifactId, and version in pom.xml.
2. Ensure the plugin exists on Maven Central or add the correct plugin repository.
3. For Spring Boot, use 'org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-maven-plugin'.

errors.rootCause: The Maven compilation phase failed. Check the detailed error messages above this line for the specific Java/Kotlin compilation errors.
errors.prescription:
1. Look for the actual compiler errors in the output above this message.
2. Fix the compiler errors (missing imports, type mismatches, etc.).
3. The 'Compilation failure' message is a summary — the root cause is in the preceding compiler output.

errors.rootCause: Gradle found conflicting versions of a dependency between '{dep1}' and '{dep2}'. Multiple modules require different versions of the same library.
errors.prescription:
1. Use a resolution strategy in build.gradle to force a specific version.
2. Use a BOM (Bill of Materials) to align versions.
3. Run 'gradle dependencies' to see the full dependency tree and identify conflicts.

errors.messagePattern: (?:Could not target platform|Unsupported class file major version)\s*(?<version>\d+)?
errors.match: (?:Could not target platform|Unsupported class file major version)
errors.rootCause: The project's target Java version does not match the JDK being used to compile. Class file version {version} is not supported by the current JDK.
errors.prescription:
1. Set sourceCompatibility and targetCompatibility in build.gradle to match your JDK.
2. Install the correct JDK version.
3. Set JAVA_HOME to point to the correct JDK.
4. In Gradle, use toolchains: java { toolchain { languageVersion.set(JavaLanguageVersion.of(17)) } }

categories.name: Generics and Annotation Errors
categories.description: Errors related to generic types, wildcards, and annotation processing

errors.rootCause: Java does not allow creating arrays of parameterized types due to type erasure. 'new T[size]' or 'new List<String>[size]' are illegal.
errors.prescription:
1. Use a Collection instead of an array: List<T> instead of T[].
2. Use Array.newInstance() with a cast: (T[]) Array.newInstance(clazz, size).
3. Use an Object[] with @SuppressWarnings("unchecked").

errors.rootCause: Raw type '{typeName}' is used without type parameters. This bypasses generic type safety and can cause ClassCastException at runtime.
errors.prescription:
1. Add the appropriate type parameter: {typeName}<Type> instead of raw {typeName}.
2. Use diamond inference: new {typeName}<>() in Java 7+.
3. If the type parameter is unknown, use {typeName}<?>.

errors.messagePattern: annotation (?:type )?(?<annotName>[\w.@]+)\s+(?:is )?not applicable to this kind of declaration
errors.rootCause: Annotation '{annotName}' is being applied to a declaration type it does not support. Annotations have @Target restrictions.
errors.prescription:
1. Check the @Target of '{annotName}' to see where it can be applied (TYPE, METHOD, FIELD, PARAMETER, etc.).
2. Move the annotation to a valid location.
3. If you need this behavior, look for an alternative annotation that targets the correct element type.

errors.messagePattern: annotation\s+(?<annotName>[\w.@]+)\s+is missing (?:a default value for )?(?:the element|element) '(?<element>\w+)'
errors.rootCause: Annotation '{annotName}' requires element '{element}' which has no default value and was not provided.
errors.prescription:
1. Provide the required element: @{annotName}({element} = value).
2. If the annotation has only one required element named 'value', you can use the shorthand: @{annotName}(value).

errors.rootCause: An annotation processor crashed or produced an error during compilation. This is common with Lombok, Dagger, MapStruct, and similar code-generation tools.
errors.prescription:
1. Check that the annotation processor dependency is correctly declared (annotationProcessor or kapt).
2. Clean and rebuild: 'gradle clean build'.
3. Check if the processor version is compatible with your Java version.
4. Look at the full stack trace for the specific processor that failed.

errors.messagePattern: (?:Possible heap pollution from parameterized vararg|unchecked generic array creation for varargs)
errors.rootCause: Using a generic type as a varargs parameter can cause heap pollution. The varargs argument is implemented as an array, and generic arrays are unsafe in Java.
errors.prescription:
1. Add @SafeVarargs annotation if the method does not store into the varargs array.
2. Add @SuppressWarnings("unchecked") if you are confident in safety.
3. Accept a List<T> instead of T... to avoid the issue entirely.

categories.name: Records, Sealed Types, and Modern Java Errors
categories.description: Errors specific to Java 16+ records and Java 17+ sealed types

errors.messagePattern: record component accessor method '(?<methodName>\w+)' must return '(?<expectedType>[\w.<>]+)'
errors.rootCause: The record component accessor method '{methodName}' must return type '{expectedType}' matching the component declaration.
errors.prescription:
1. Change the return type of '{methodName}' to '{expectedType}'.
2. Records auto-generate accessor methods — only override them if you need custom behavior.
3. Ensure the method name matches the component name exactly.

errors.rootCause: Java records implicitly extend java.lang.Record and cannot extend other classes. Records are designed to be simple data carriers.
errors.prescription:
1. Use an interface instead of a class for shared behavior: record MyRecord(...) implements MyInterface.
2. Convert to a regular class if you need class inheritance.
3. Use composition: include the parent class as a component of the record.

errors.messagePattern: the switch expression does not cover all possible input values
errors.match: error:.*does not cover all possible input values
errors.rootCause: A switch expression must be exhaustive — it must cover all possible values of the input type. For sealed classes, all permitted subclasses must be handled.
errors.prescription:
1. Add cases for all missing variants.
2. Add a 'default' case to handle any remaining values.
3. For sealed types, ensure all permitted subtypes have a case.

categories.name: Miscellaneous Java Errors
categories.description: Other common Java compilation warnings and errors

errors.rootCause: Static member '{memberName}' in '{className}' is being accessed through an instance reference. While this works, it is misleading and should use the class name.
errors.prescription: Use the class name to access static members: {className}.{memberName} instead of instance.{memberName}.

errors.rootCause: Integer division by zero will throw ArithmeticException at runtime.
errors.prescription:
1. Add a check for zero before dividing.
2. Use a default value when the divisor is zero.
3. Consider if the divisor can legitimately be zero in your business logic.

errors.rootCause: The cast to '{targetType}' is unnecessary — the expression already has this type.
errors.prescription: Remove the redundant cast. The value is already of type '{targetType}'.

errors.rootCause: The finally block contains a return or throw statement, which will silently swallow any exception thrown in the try block. This is almost always a bug.
errors.prescription:
1. Remove the return/throw from the finally block.
2. Move the return to outside the try-finally structure.
3. The finally block should only contain cleanup code (closing resources).

errors.rootCause: A switch case is missing a break statement and will fall through to the next case. In most situations this is unintentional.
errors.prescription:
1. Add a 'break;' at the end of the case.
2. Use switch expressions (Java 14+) which do not fall through.
3. If fall-through is intentional, add a comment: // fall through.

errors.rootCause: Class '{className}' implements Serializable but does not declare a serialVersionUID field. This can cause deserialization failures when the class changes.
errors.prescription: Add a serialVersionUID field: private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;

aliasConfig.format: Source directories define the root for package-to-directory resolution. A class 'com.example.Foo' maps to 'src/main/java/com/example/Foo.java'.

moduleResolution.relativeImportRules: Java and Kotlin do not support relative imports. All imports are fully qualified package paths. The file's package declaration determines its location in the source tree.
moduleResolution.absoluteImportRules: Imports are resolved by mapping the fully-qualified class name to a file path: com.example.Foo maps to com/example/Foo.java relative to a source root. Source roots are defined in build.gradle (sourceSets) or pom.xml (build.sourceDirectory). The compiler also searches the classpath (dependencies) and the JDK standard library.

definitionPatterns.example: public sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle {
public enum UserRole { ADMIN, USER, GUEST }
public record Point(int x, int y) {
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)

inheritancePatterns.example: sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle {
class UserServiceImpl(private val repo: UserRepo) : UserService, Loggable {
data class Success(val data: String) : Result()

typeDeclarationLocations.description: Gradle build output for project modules

detectionRules.condition: Import references a package that is not provided by any declared dependency or the JDK
suggestedActions: Add the dependency to build.gradle or pom.xml
Search Maven Central for the package

detectionRules.condition: Package exists but a specific class or method is not found — likely a version mismatch
suggestedActions: Review the library's changelog for API changes

detectionRules.condition: Generated code (from Lombok, Dagger, MapStruct) is not found
suggestedActions: For Kotlin, use kapt or ksp instead

detectionRules.condition: Class from a sibling module in a multi-module project is not found
Package starting with javax.* or sun.* not found — removed from JDK 11+
suggestedActions: Add the Jakarta EE dependency explicitly
Migrate from javax.* to jakarta.* for Jakarta EE 9+

commonBreakingPatterns.description: javax to jakarta namespace migration (Jakarta EE 9+)
commonBreakingPatterns.resolution: Replace javax.* imports with jakarta.* equivalents and update dependencies to Jakarta EE 9+ artifacts

commonBreakingPatterns.description: Gradle api vs implementation scope — transitive dependency not exposed
commonBreakingPatterns.resolution: Change 'implementation' to 'api' in the module that declares the dependency, or add the dependency directly to the consuming module

commonBreakingPatterns.description: Kotlin version mismatch between plugin and stdlib
commonBreakingPatterns.resolution: Align kotlin-stdlib version with the Kotlin Gradle plugin version in build.gradle.kts

commonBreakingPatterns.description: Annotation processor not running — generated code missing
commonBreakingPatterns.resolution: Add annotationProcessor (Java) or kapt/ksp (Kotlin) dependency. Clean and rebuild.

commonBreakingPatterns.description: Spring Boot auto-configuration class not found after version upgrade
commonBreakingPatterns.resolution: Check Spring Boot migration guide for relocated or renamed auto-configuration classes

quirks.name: Annotation Processing Code Generation
quirks.description: Annotation processors (Lombok, Dagger, MapStruct, JPA Metamodel) generate source code during compilation. The generated classes do not exist in the source tree but are referenced by user code. Build tools place generated sources in build/generated/sources/annotationProcessor/.
quirks.workaround: Check build/generated/ directories for generated source files. If a 'cannot find symbol' error references a class with a pattern like *_Generated, *Impl, *Builder, *Mapper, or *_, it is likely annotation-processor generated. Ensure the annotation processor dependency is correctly declared.

quirks.name: Spring Boot Auto-Configuration
quirks.description: Spring Boot auto-configuration creates beans at runtime based on classpath scanning and @ConditionalOn* annotations. Dependencies between beans are invisible at compile time. Spring also uses proxy classes generated at runtime.
quirks.workaround: When errors involve @Autowired fields, @Bean methods, or @Component classes, check that the Spring context is configured correctly. Missing beans are runtime errors, not compile errors. However, missing Spring Boot starters can cause compile errors when their auto-configured APIs are referenced.

quirks.name: Kotlin/Java Interop
quirks.description: Kotlin and Java can call each other, but there are friction points: Kotlin's null safety vs Java's nullable-by-default, companion objects, default parameter values, extension functions, and property accessors. Java sees Kotlin companion members through a Companion static field. Kotlin sees Java types as platform types (T!) with unknown nullability.
quirks.workaround: When tracing between .kt and .java files: (1) Add @JvmStatic, @JvmField, @JvmOverloads annotations for better Java interop. (2) Add @Nullable/@NonNull annotations to Java code for Kotlin null safety. (3) Kotlin extension functions appear as static methods with the receiver as the first parameter in Java.

quirks.name: Multi-Module Gradle Projects
quirks.description: Gradle multi-module projects use settings.gradle to include modules and build.gradle in each module to declare dependencies. The 'api' vs 'implementation' scope controls whether transitive dependencies are visible to consumer modules. This is the most common source of 'cannot find symbol' errors in multi-module projects.
quirks.workaround: Parse settings.gradle(.kts) to find all included modules. For each module's build.gradle(.kts), extract implementation() and api() dependencies. Only api() dependencies are transitively visible. When a class is not found, check if it comes from a transitive implementation() dependency.

quirks.name: Source Sets (main, test, integration)
quirks.description: Gradle source sets separate main code, test code, and other categories. Test source sets can see main classes but not vice versa. Custom source sets (e.g., integrationTest) need explicit dependency configuration.
quirks.workaround: When a class is not found, check which source set the referencing file is in. Test files are in src/test/java and can access src/main/java. Integration test files may need additional source set configuration to access main or test classes.

quirks.name: Circular Module Dependencies
quirks.description: Gradle modules cannot have circular dependencies (A depends on B, B depends on A). This is a hard error. Within a single module, circular class dependencies are allowed but can cause initialization issues.
quirks.workaround: If a circular dependency is detected, extract the shared interface or model into a third module that both A and B depend on. Within a module, circular class references are resolved at compile time but may cause problems with annotation processors.

quirks.name: Kotlin Multiplatform Expect/Actual
quirks.description: Kotlin Multiplatform uses 'expect' declarations in common code and 'actual' implementations in platform-specific source sets. Missing 'actual' declarations cause compilation errors.
quirks.workaround: For 'expect' without 'actual' errors, find the expect declaration in commonMain and add actual implementations in each platform source set (jvmMain, androidMain, iosMain, etc.).

quirks.name: Kapt vs KSP for Kotlin Annotation Processing
quirks.description: Kotlin has two annotation processing systems: kapt (legacy, wraps Java APT) and KSP (Kotlin Symbol Processing, newer and faster). Some processors only support one. Using the wrong one causes missing generated code.
quirks.workaround: Check whether the annotation processor supports KSP. If so, use ksp() instead of kapt(). If generated code is missing, ensure the correct processor plugin (id 'com.google.devtools.ksp' or id 'org.jetbrains.kotlin.kapt') is applied in build.gradle.kts.

quirks.name: Gradle Build Cache Staleness
quirks.description: Gradle's build cache can become stale after changing dependencies, annotation processor versions, or Kotlin/Java versions. Stale cache causes phantom compilation errors where generated code or compiled classes are outdated.
quirks.workaround: If errors seem inconsistent or appear after dependency changes, run 'gradle clean build --no-build-cache' to force a fresh compilation. For persistent issues, delete the .gradle/ and build/ directories.
quirks.example:
# Clean everything and rebuild
gradle clean build --no-build-cache

# Nuclear option — delete all caches
rm -rf .gradle/ build/ */build/
